Python Basics.question: What is a list?
Python Basics.answer: An ordered, mutable collection of items

Python Basics.question: What is a dictionary?
Python Basics.answer: A collection of key-value pairs

Python Basics.question: What does len() do?
Python Basics.answer: Returns the number of items in an object

Python Basics.question: What is a list comprehension?
Python Basics.answer: A concise way to create lists: [x for x in iterable]

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between append() and extend()?
Python Basics.answer: append() adds a single item, extend() adds all items from an iterable

Python Basics.question: What is a lambda function?
Python Basics.answer: An anonymous one-line function defined with the lambda keyword

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between == and is?
Python Basics.answer: == checks value equality, is checks if two variables point to the same object

Python Basics.question: What is a tuple?
Python Basics.answer: An ordered, immutable collection of items

Python Basics.question: What does the pass keyword do?
Python Basics.answer: Acts as a placeholder that does nothing

Python Basics.question: What is a set?
Python Basics.answer: An unordered collection of unique items

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between a list and a tuple?
Python Basics.answer: Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable

Python Basics.question: What does the zip() function do?
Python Basics.answer: Combines multiple iterables into tuples element by element

Python Basics.question: What is a decorator?
Python Basics.answer: A function that wraps another function to modify its behavior

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between *args and **kwargs?
Python Basics.answer: *args passes variable positional arguments, **kwargs passes variable keyword arguments

Python Basics.question: What is a generator?
Python Basics.answer: A function that yields values one at a time using the yield keyword instead of return

Python Basics.question: What does enumerate() do?
Python Basics.answer: Returns an iterator of tuples containing the index and value of each item

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy?
Python Basics.answer: Shallow copy copies references, deep copy copies the actual objects recursively

Python Basics.question: What is None in Python?
Python Basics.answer: A special constant representing the absence of a value

Python Basics.question: What does the map() function do?
Python Basics.answer: Applies a function to every item in an iterable and returns a map object

Python Basics.question: What is exception handling?
Python Basics.answer: Using try/except blocks to catch and handle errors gracefully

Python Basics.question: What is the global keyword?
Python Basics.answer: Declares that a variable inside a function refers to a globally scoped variable

Python Basics.question: What is a module in Python?
Python Basics.answer: A file containing Python code that can be imported and used in other files

Python Basics.question: What does the filter() function do?
Python Basics.answer: Filters items from an iterable based on a function that returns True or False

Python Basics.question: What is the difference between break and continue?
Python Basics.answer: break exits the loop entirely, continue skips to the next iteration

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is Big-O notation?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A way to describe time or space complexity as input size grows

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the time complexity of binary search?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: O(log n)

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a linked list?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A linear data structure where each element points to the next via a pointer

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a stack?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A data structure that follows Last In First Out (LIFO) order

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a queue?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A data structure that follows First In First Out (FIFO) order

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an array element?
What is a hash table?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function for O(1) average lookups

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a binary tree?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A tree where each node has at most two children

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: BFS explores level by level using a queue, DFS explores depth-first using a stack or recursion

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the time complexity of quicksort on average?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: O(n log n)

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a heap?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A tree-based data structure where the parent is always greater than or less than its children

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is dynamic programming?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: Breaking a problem into subproblems and storing results to avoid redundant computation

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is memoization?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: Caching the results of expensive function calls to avoid recomputation

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the time complexity of inserting into a hash table?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: O(1) on average

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a graph?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A data structure of nodes connected by edges, can be directed or undirected

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the difference between a tree and a graph?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A tree is a graph with no cycles and a single root node

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is bubble sort?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A sorting algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order, O(n^2)

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is merge sort?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A divide and conquer sorting algorithm with O(n log n) time complexity

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a trie?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A tree data structure used to store strings where each node represents a character

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a two pointer technique?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: Using two pointers to traverse an array simultaneously to solve problems efficiently

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is a sliding window?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A technique that maintains a subset of data within a window that slides across an array

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is recursion?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A function that calls itself until a base case is reached

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the space complexity of a recursive function?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: O(n) due to the call stack growing with each recursive call

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is an adjacency list?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: A way to represent a graph where each node stores a list of its neighbors

Data Structures & Algorithms.question: What is the difference between a min heap and a max heap?
Data Structures & Algorithms.answer: Min heap: smallest element at root. Max heap: largest element at root

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is encapsulation?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Bundling data and methods within a class and restricting direct access from outside

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is inheritance?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A mechanism where a class derives properties and methods from a parent class

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is polymorphism?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: The ability of different objects to respond to the same method in different ways

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is abstraction?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Hiding complex implementation details and exposing only necessary features

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is a constructor?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A special method called when an object is instantiated to initialize its attributes

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the difference between a class and an object?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A class is a blueprint, an object is an instance of that class

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is method overriding?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Redefining a parent class method in a child class to change its behavior

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is method overloading?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is an interface?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A contract that defines methods a class must implement without providing implementation

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is an abstract class?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods subclasses must implement

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the difference between public, private, and protected?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Public: accessible anywhere. Private: accessible only within the class. Protected: accessible within class and subclasses

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is a static method?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A method that belongs to the class rather than an instance and cannot access instance variables

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is composition?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Building complex objects by combining simpler objects rather than using inheritance

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the difference between composition and inheritance?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Inheritance is an is-a relationship, composition is a has-a relationship

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is a destructor?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A method called when an object is destroyed to release resources

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is coupling?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: The degree of dependency between classes, lower coupling is better for maintainability

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is cohesion?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: How closely related the responsibilities of a class are, higher cohesion is better

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the Single Responsibility Principle?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one job

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the Open/Closed Principle?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is a design pattern?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software design

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the Singleton pattern?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A design pattern that ensures only one instance of a class exists

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the Factory pattern?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A design pattern that creates objects without specifying the exact class to instantiate

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the Observer pattern?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: A design pattern where objects subscribe to events and get notified when they occur

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is duck typing?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: If an object has the required methods and properties, it can be used regardless of its type

Object Oriented Programming.question: What is the difference between an instance variable and a class variable?
Object Oriented Programming.answer: Instance variables are unique to each object, class variables are shared across all instances

Web Development.question: What is HTTP?
Web Development.answer: HyperText Transfer Protocol, the foundation of data communication on the web

Web Development.question: What is the difference between GET and POST?
Web Development.answer: GET retrieves data, POST sends data to the server to create or update a resource

Web Development.question: What is a REST API?
Web Development.answer: An API that uses HTTP methods and follows stateless communication principles

Web Development.question: What is JSON?
Web Development.answer: JavaScript Object Notation, a lightweight format for storing and transporting data

Web Development.question: What is the DOM?
Web Development.answer: Document Object Model, a tree representation of HTML elements that JavaScript can manipulate

Web Development.question: What is the difference between HTML, CSS, and JavaScript?
Web Development.answer: HTML is structure, CSS is styling, JavaScript is behavior

Web Development.question: What is a 404 error?
Web Development.answer: A HTTP status code meaning the requested resource was not found

Web Development.question: What is a 500 error?
Web Development.answer: A HTTP status code meaning an internal server error occurred

Web Development.question: What is CORS?
Web Development.answer: Cross Origin Resource Sharing, a security feature that controls which domains can access an API

Web Development.question: What is a cookie?
Web Development.answer: A small piece of data stored in the browser used to remember information between sessions

Web Development.question: What is a session?
Web Development.answer: Server-side storage of user data that persists across multiple requests

Web Development.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
Web Development.answer: Authentication verifies who you are, authorization determines what you can access

Web Development.question: What is JWT?
Web Development.answer: JSON Web Token, a compact way to securely transmit information between parties as a JSON object

Web Development.question: What is a CDN?
Web Development.answer: Content Delivery Network, a distributed network of servers that delivers content faster based on location

Web Development.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL?
Web Development.answer: SQL uses structured tables with schemas, NoSQL uses flexible document or key-value storage

Web Development.question: What is an ORM?
Web Development.answer: Object Relational Mapper, a tool that lets you interact with a database using objects instead of SQL

Web Development.question: What is caching?
Web Development.answer: Storing frequently accessed data temporarily to reduce load times and server requests

Web Development.question: What is a webhook?
Web Development.answer: A way for an app to send real-time data to another app when an event occurs

Web Development.question: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
Web Development.answer: localStorage persists after the browser closes, sessionStorage is cleared when the tab closes

Web Development.question: What is a load balancer?
Web Development.answer: A server that distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload

Web Development.question: What is responsive design?
Web Development.answer: Designing web pages that adapt their layout to different screen sizes

Web Development.question: What is a microservice?
Web Development.answer: An architectural style where an app is built as small independent services that communicate via APIs

Web Development.question: What is CI/CD?
Web Development.answer: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment, automating the process of testing and deploying code

Web Development.question: What is Docker?
Web Development.answer: A platform that packages applications into containers so they run consistently across environments

Web Development.question: What is an index in a database?
Web Development.answer: A data structure that speeds up data retrieval by allowing faster lookups on specific columns

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is supervised learning?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Training a model on labeled data where the correct output is already known

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is unsupervised learning?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Training a model on unlabeled data to find hidden patterns or groupings

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a neural network?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A model inspired by the brain consisting of layers of interconnected nodes that learn from data

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is overfitting?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: When a model performs well on training data but poorly on new unseen data

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is underfitting?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: When a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a training set vs test set?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Training set is used to train the model, test set is used to evaluate its performance on unseen data

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is gradient descent?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: An optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by iteratively adjusting model parameters

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a loss function?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A function that measures how far a model's predictions are from the actual values

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a hyperparameter?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A parameter set before training that controls the learning process, such as learning rate or batch size

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is transfer learning?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Reusing a pre-trained model on a new but related task to save time and data

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a convolutional neural network?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A neural network designed for image processing that uses convolutional layers to detect features

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a large language model?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A neural network trained on massive amounts of text data to understand and generate language

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is reinforcement learning?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Training an agent to make decisions by rewarding desired behaviors and penalizing undesired ones

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a confusion matrix?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A table that summarizes the performance of a classification model showing true/false positives and negatives

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is precision?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: The proportion of positive predictions that were actually correct

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is recall?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: The proportion of actual positives that were correctly identified by the model

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is feature engineering?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: The process of selecting and transforming raw data into features that improve model performance

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is normalization?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: Scaling input data to a standard range, typically 0 to 1, to improve model training

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a decision tree?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A model that splits data into branches based on feature values to make predictions

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is random forest?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: An ensemble of decision trees that combines their predictions to improve accuracy

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is k-means clustering?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: An unsupervised algorithm that groups data into k clusters based on similarity

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is a transformer model?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A neural network architecture that uses attention mechanisms, foundational to modern LLMs

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is attention in machine learning?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A mechanism that allows a model to focus on the most relevant parts of the input when making predictions

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is cross-validation?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A technique that evaluates a model by training and testing on different subsets of data multiple times

AI & Machine Learning.question: What is regularization?
AI & Machine Learning.answer: A technique that adds a penalty to the loss function to reduce overfitting by discouraging complex models

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the time complexity of finding the largest product of three numbers in an array?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: O(n) — sort or use a single pass tracking the three largest and two smallest values

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is dynamic programming and when should you use it?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: Breaking a problem into overlapping subproblems and caching results. Use it when a problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a DP on graph problem?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A problem that combines graph traversal with dynamic programming, such as finding the longest path in a DAG or shortest path with constraints

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a REST API?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: An API that uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and stateless communication to transfer data between client and server

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is concurrency and what problems can it cause?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: Concurrency is multiple tasks executing overlapping in time. Problems include race conditions, deadlocks, and starvation

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a deadlock?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A situation where two or more threads are each waiting for the other to release a resource, causing all to be stuck indefinitely

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a race condition?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: When two threads access shared data simultaneously and the outcome depends on the order of execution

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a mutex?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A mutual exclusion lock that prevents multiple threads from accessing a shared resource simultaneously

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A process is an independent program with its own memory. A thread is a unit of execution within a process that shares memory with other threads

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a SQL JOIN?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A SQL operation that combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: INNER JOIN returns only matching rows. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is an index in a database and why does it matter?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A data structure that speeds up lookups on a column. Without an index, queries do a full table scan which is O(n)

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the stocks problem in dynamic programming?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A classic DP problem where you find the maximum profit from buying and selling stocks given price history and constraints on transactions

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is operating system virtual memory?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A memory management technique that gives each process the illusion of having its own large contiguous memory space by mapping virtual addresses to physical addresses

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a page fault?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: When a program accesses a memory page not currently in RAM, triggering the OS to load it from disk

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is ACID in databases?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — properties that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between stack and heap memory?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: Stack is used for local variables and function calls and is automatically managed. Heap is used for dynamic allocation and must be manually managed

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is a binary search tree?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A tree where each node's left subtree contains values less than the node and right subtree contains values greater, enabling O(log n) search

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is the time complexity of quicksort in the worst case?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: O(n^2) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element, such as on an already sorted array

D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.question: What is tail recursion?
D.E. Shaw | Software Engineer.answer: A recursive function where the recursive call is the last operation, allowing the compiler to optimize it into a loop to avoid stack overflow

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the probability that 3 random points on a circle are all within the same semicircle?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: 3/4 — fix one point, the other two must each fall within the semicircle starting at that point, giving (1/2)^2 * 3 = 3/4

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the Central Limit Theorem?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: The distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the population distribution

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is Value at Risk (VaR)?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A statistical measure of the maximum potential loss of a portfolio over a given time period at a given confidence level

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is a derivative in finance?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A financial instrument whose value is derived from an underlying asset, index, or rate. Examples include options, futures, and swaps

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the difference between a call and put option?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A call gives the right to buy an asset at a set price. A put gives the right to sell an asset at a set price

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the Black-Scholes model?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A mathematical model for pricing European options using variables including stock price, strike price, volatility, time to expiry, and risk-free rate

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is Bayes theorem?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: P(A|B) = P(B|A) * P(A) / P(B). It updates the probability of an event given new evidence

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the expected value of a fair die roll?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: 3.5 — the average of 1 through 6 is (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is mean reversion?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: The tendency of asset prices or returns to revert to their long-term historical average over time

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the Sharpe ratio?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A measure of risk-adjusted return calculated as (portfolio return minus risk-free rate) divided by portfolio standard deviation

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the law of large numbers?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: As the number of trials increases, the sample mean converges to the expected value

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is a Monte Carlo simulation?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A technique that uses random sampling to model the probability of different outcomes in a process with uncertainty

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is alpha in finance?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: The excess return of a portfolio relative to a benchmark, representing the value added by the portfolio manager

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is beta in finance?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A measure of a stock's volatility relative to the market. Beta of 1 means the stock moves with the market

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is standard deviation and why does it matter in finance?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A measure of how spread out returns are from the mean. In finance it represents risk or volatility of an asset

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is the difference between systematic and unsystematic risk?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: Systematic risk affects the entire market and cannot be diversified away. Unsystematic risk is company-specific and can be reduced through diversification

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is covariance and how is it used in portfolio construction?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: Covariance measures how two assets move together. Low or negative covariance between assets reduces overall portfolio risk

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: If you flip a fair coin 1000 times, what theorem describes the probability of getting more than 550 heads?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: The Central Limit Theorem. The number of heads is approximately normal with mean 500 and standard deviation ~15.8, so 550 is about 3.16 standard deviations above the mean

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is arbitrage?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: The simultaneous purchase and sale of the same asset in different markets to profit from a price difference with no risk

D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.question: What is a hedge fund and how does D.E. Shaw differ from a traditional hedge fund?
D.E. Shaw | Quantitative Analyst.answer: A hedge fund pools capital to generate returns. D.E. Shaw is distinguished by its quantitative and computational approach, using mathematical models and algorithms rather than fundamental analysis

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS and when would you use each?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: BFS uses a queue and finds shortest paths. DFS uses a stack or recursion and is better for exploring all paths or detecting cycles

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you design a URL shortener like bit.ly?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Use a hash function to generate a short code, store the mapping in a database, and redirect users via a GET request. Consider caching, rate limiting, and expiration

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A greedy algorithm that finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges, in O((V+E) log V)

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between merge sort and quicksort?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Merge sort is O(n log n) always and stable but uses extra space. Quicksort is O(n log n) average but O(n^2) worst case and sorts in place

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you design a distributed messenger system?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Use websockets for real-time messaging, a message queue for delivery guarantees, a database for persistence, and horizontal scaling with load balancers

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the time complexity of searching in a hash map?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: O(1) average case, O(n) worst case due to hash collisions

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is memoization and how does it differ from tabulation?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Memoization is top-down DP that caches recursive results. Tabulation is bottom-up DP that fills a table iteratively. Both avoid recomputation

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you find the kth largest element in an array?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Use a min-heap of size k in O(n log k), or use quickselect for O(n) average time

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is a trie and what is it used for?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A tree where each node represents a character. Used for efficient prefix searches, autocomplete, and spell checking in O(m) where m is the word length

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you detect a cycle in a linked list?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Use Floyd's cycle detection algorithm — two pointers moving at different speeds. If they meet, there is a cycle

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the two pointer technique?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Using two indices that move toward each other or in the same direction to solve array problems in O(n) instead of O(n^2)

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is a sliding window algorithm?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A technique that maintains a window of elements that slides across an array, useful for problems involving contiguous subarrays or substrings

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you reverse a linked list?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Iteratively use three pointers (prev, current, next) to reverse each link. O(n) time and O(1) space

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A stack is LIFO — last in first out. A queue is FIFO — first in first out

Google | Software Engineer.question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with a teammate. How did you resolve it?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Use the STAR method — describe the Situation, Task, Action, and Result. Focus on listening, finding common ground, and reaching a data-driven decision

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is load balancing and why is it important?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Distributing incoming requests across multiple servers to prevent any one server from being overwhelmed, improving availability and performance

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is the CAP theorem?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A distributed system can only guarantee two of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you find all anagrams of a string in a list of strings?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Sort each string's characters and use a hash map to group strings with the same sorted form. O(n * k log k) where k is string length

Google | Software Engineer.question: What is a heap and what are its common uses?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: A tree-based data structure satisfying the heap property. Used for priority queues, finding kth largest/smallest elements, and heap sort

Google | Software Engineer.question: How would you check if a binary tree is balanced?
Google | Software Engineer.answer: Recursively check that the height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most 1. O(n) time

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Concurrency is tasks making progress in overlapping time periods. Parallelism is tasks literally executing at the same instant on multiple cores

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is memory management in iOS?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: iOS uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) which automatically tracks and manages memory by counting strong references to objects

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is a retain cycle and how do you fix it?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: When two objects hold strong references to each other preventing deallocation. Fixed by making one reference weak or unowned

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between a struct and a class in Swift?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Structs are value types copied on assignment. Classes are reference types sharing the same instance. Structs do not support inheritance

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the delegate pattern?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: A design pattern where one object delegates responsibilities to another object conforming to a protocol, common in iOS for handling events

Apple | Software Engineer.question: How would you design a settings synchronization service across Apple devices?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Use iCloud key-value storage for small settings, conflict resolution with timestamps, and background sync with retry logic for network failures

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is Grand Central Dispatch (GCD)?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Apple's concurrency framework that manages thread pools and allows you to dispatch tasks to serial or concurrent queues

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between a serial and concurrent queue in GCD?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: A serial queue executes one task at a time in order. A concurrent queue executes multiple tasks simultaneously

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the MVC design pattern?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Model View Controller — separates an app into data (Model), UI (View), and logic that connects them (Controller)

Apple | Software Engineer.question: Tell me about a time you took a project end to end with minimal direction.
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Use STAR method. Highlight ownership, decisions made independently, how you handled ambiguity, and the impact of the outcome

Apple | Software Engineer.question: How would you handle code quality under pressure and tight deadlines?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Prioritize critical paths, write tests for core functionality, communicate tradeoffs to stakeholders, and schedule time to address technical debt post-release

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: SQL databases use structured tables and schemas with ACID guarantees. NoSQL databases use flexible document or key-value storage optimized for scale and speed

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is caching and what problem does it solve?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Storing frequently accessed data in fast memory to reduce latency and database load. Solves slow repeated reads of the same data

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the SOLID principle?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Five OOP design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion

Apple | Software Engineer.question: How would you approach debugging a production crash with no reproduction steps?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Analyze crash logs and stack traces, check recent code changes, add logging around suspect areas, reproduce in staging, and use crash reporting tools like Firebase

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the observer pattern?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: A design pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them of state changes. Used in iOS via NotificationCenter and KVO

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous execution?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Synchronous blocks the current thread until the task completes. Asynchronous submits the task and returns immediately, continuing execution

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is Big O notation?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: A way to describe the time or space complexity of an algorithm as the input size grows, ignoring constants and lower order terms

Apple | Software Engineer.question: How do you ensure privacy in system design at Apple?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: Minimize data collection, process data on-device where possible, encrypt data in transit and at rest, and give users clear control over their data

Apple | Software Engineer.question: What is the difference between a deep copy and a shallow copy?
Apple | Software Engineer.answer: A shallow copy copies references to objects. A deep copy recursively copies all objects so changes to one do not affect the other

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a GPU and how does it differ from a CPU?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A GPU has thousands of smaller cores optimized for parallel processing. A CPU has fewer but more powerful cores optimized for sequential tasks

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is CUDA?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and programming model that allows developers to use GPUs for general purpose processing

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a finite state machine?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A computational model with a finite number of states, transitions between states, and actions. Used extensively in digital design and hardware control logic

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a Karnaugh map (K-map)?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A visual method for simplifying Boolean algebra expressions to minimize the number of logic gates needed in a circuit

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is the difference between combinational and sequential logic?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: Combinational logic output depends only on current inputs. Sequential logic output depends on current inputs and past states stored in memory elements

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a flip-flop?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A basic memory element in digital circuits that stores one bit of state and changes output on a clock edge

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is power sequencing?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: The controlled order in which power rails are enabled during system startup to ensure hardware components receive power in the correct sequence

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is PCIe?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: Peripheral Component Interconnect Express — a high speed serial bus interface used to connect GPUs, SSDs, and other hardware to a motherboard

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is the difference between SRAM and DRAM?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: SRAM is faster and does not need refreshing but is more expensive. DRAM is slower and needs constant refreshing but is cheaper and denser

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a System on Chip (SoC)?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: An integrated circuit that combines all components of a computer including CPU, GPU, memory, and I/O onto a single chip

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is memory bandwidth and why does it matter for GPUs?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: The rate at which data can be read from or written to memory. GPUs need high memory bandwidth to feed thousands of cores with data simultaneously

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a pipeline in computer architecture?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A technique where multiple instruction stages are overlapped in execution, similar to an assembly line, to increase instruction throughput

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is cache coherence?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: The consistency of shared data stored in multiple local caches of a multi-processor system

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is thermal design power (TDP)?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: The maximum amount of heat a chip is expected to generate under sustained load, used to design cooling solutions

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is DFS traversal on a graph?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: Depth First Search explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a stack or recursion

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is the difference between big endian and little endian?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: Big endian stores the most significant byte first. Little endian stores the least significant byte first

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is a voltage regulator?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A circuit that maintains a constant output voltage regardless of changes in input voltage or load current

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is the purpose of a clock signal in digital circuits?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: To synchronize the operations of all components in a circuit, ensuring data is captured and transferred at the correct time

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is VHDL or Verilog?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: Hardware description languages used to model and simulate digital circuits before physical implementation

NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.question: What is the von Neumann architecture?
NVIDIA | Hardware Engineer.answer: A computer architecture where CPU, memory, and I/O share a single bus, with instructions and data stored in the same memory
